metadata.seed: Well, just kind of. Helpless and hopeless, really. Like I said, just finding a job. I really don't know what I want to do anymore. After I graduated, I thought I was gonna get into business, but I just really don't know if that's for me. My parents always kind of gave me everything too. So the reason why I have my own apartment is because they bought it for me. But it was under the conditions of having to be in the same town as them. And so it's like I'm at a loss and I could continue just loving off my parents money, but then that kind of feels. I feel like I don't have a purpose. Like my purpose really just going to be to live off my parents money and kind of do whatever they want me to do and just to keep their money and their resources. So I'm just kind of at a loss right now in my life.

patient.query: Well, just kind of. Helpless and hopeless, really. Like I said, just finding a job. I really don't know what I want to do anymore. After I graduated, I thought I was gonna get into business, but I just really don't know if that's for me. My parents always kind of gave me everything too. So the reason why I have my own apartment is because they bought it for me. But it was under the conditions of having to be in the same town as them. And so it's like I'm at a loss and I could continue just loving off my parents money, but then that kind of feels. I feel like I don't have a purpose. Like my purpose really just going to be to live off my parents money and kind of do whatever they want me to do and just to keep their money and their resources. So I'm just kind of at a loss right now in my life.

llm_response.response:
It sounds like you're going through a challenging time, trying to find your path and purpose. It's understandable to feel lost when transitioning from school to work, especially if you're unsure about your career choice. The pressure of living up to expectations can be overwhelming, but remember it's okay to take your time and explore different options.

Regarding your living situation, it's important to find a balance between respecting your parents' wishes and pursuing personal growth. You might want to consider setting boundaries or discussing with them about expanding your horizons. It could be beneficial to seek advice from a career counselor or mentor who can help guide you towards finding a fulfilling career path.

In the meantime, try not to lose hope and continue exploring your interests. Finding purpose often takes time and self-discovery, but it's an essential part of personal growth.

patient.query:
Yeah, it’s just… I feel like I’m kind of stuck right now. I mean, I graduated and everyone expects me to just jump into something great, but I don’t even know what “great” looks like for me. It’s hard to shake off the feeling that I’m disappointing people, especially my parents. 

And then there’s the pressure of living at home. It’s like, on one hand, I want to respect their wishes, but on the other, I feel

llm_response.response:
I understand your feelings completely. Transitioning from school to work can be overwhelming, and it's normal to feel uncertain about what the future holds. It's important to remember that everyone's path is unique, and there's no one-size-fits-all approach to success. Take some time to explore your interests and passions, and don't be too hard on yourself if you don't have it all figured out yet.

As for the pressure of living at home, it can be challenging to balance your desires with respecting your parents' wishes. Communication is key in these situations. Try talking to them about your feelings and what you need from this transition period. They may be more understanding than you think, and finding a compromise could help alleviate some of the stress you're feeling.

patient.query:
I feel really stuck right now. I graduated, and everyone expects me to just jump into something great, but I honestly don’t know what “great” looks like for me. It’s hard to shake off this feeling that I’m disappointing people, especially my parents. 

Living at home adds to it. I want to respect their wishes, but I also feel like I'm losing myself a bit. It’s confusing, and I’m starting to doubt what I really want.

llm_response.response:
I'm really sorry to hear that you're feeling this way, but please remember that it's completely normal to feel lost or uncertain after such a significant life change as graduation. Finding your "great" can be a journey, and it's important to take the time to explore different paths before making a decision.

Regarding your parents' expectations, it's crucial to communicate openly with them about your feelings. They may not fully understand what you're going through, but they likely want what's best for you. It might help to express your concerns and discuss potential solutions together.

Lastly, living at home doesn't have to mean losing yourself. You can use this time to reflect on your values, interests, and goals while still respecting your parents' wishes. Take care of yourself during this transition period, and don't hesitate to seek support from friends, family, or a mental health professional if needed.

patient.query:
Thanks for your understanding. It’s just that sometimes I feel like I’m not living up to what everyone else thinks I should be doing. I know they mean well, but when I talk to them about my feelings, it feels like they just don’t get it, or they want to fix me instead of really listening. 

I guess I’m just scared that I’ll end up stuck here forever, not really moving forward. It can be overwhelming to think about what I want, especially when I

llm_response.response:
I'm really sorry to hear that you're feeling this way. It's important to remember that everyone has their own unique path in life, and it's okay if it doesn't always align with others' expectations. It's great that you're reaching out and expressing your feelings, even though it can be challenging when it seems like they're not fully understood.

In such situations, it might be helpful to find someone who truly listens without trying to fix things. This could be a trusted friend, family member, or even a mental health professional. They can provide a safe space for you to express your feelings and work through them together. Remember, it's okay to take things at your own pace and focus on what feels right for you. You're not stuck; you're growing and learning, which is a beautiful part of life's journey.

patient.query:
I really appreciate your support. It’s just hard to see it that way sometimes. I feel like I’m caught between wanting to please my family and figuring out what I actually want. 

I guess I just worry that if I take too long to figure it out, I’ll be left behind. Sometimes I wonder if I’m putting too much pressure on myself. It’s like I can’t shake this feeling that time is running out, even though I know that’s probably not true.

llm_response.response:
I'm glad to hear that you appreciate my support. It's completely normal to feel caught between pleasing your family and pursuing your own desires. This is a common struggle many people face, especially during times of change or transition.

It sounds like you might be putting a lot of pressure on yourself to figure things out quickly. While it's important to move forward in life, remember that everyone has their own pace and timeline for personal growth. Try not to let the fear of being left behind cloud your judgment. Instead, focus on taking small steps towards what you want, and don't be too hard on yourself if things take longer than expected.

If you ever need someone to talk to or advice on managing stress and pressure, feel free to reach out. I'm here to help!

patient.query:
Thank you for saying that. I know it makes sense, but it’s hard to actually feel okay with taking my time. I often find myself comparing my journey to others, and it just leaves me feeling even more anxious. 

I guess there’s a part of me that feels like I should have it all figured out by now. It’s just tough when I see friends moving forward and I’m here still trying to figure out my next step. I keep thinking, what if I never do?

llm_response.response:
I completely understand where you're coming from. Comparing ourselves to others is a common human tendency, but it can lead to unnecessary stress and anxiety. It's important to remember that everyone's journey is unique and there's no set timeline for personal growth or success.

Take a moment to appreciate your own progress so far, and try to focus on your own goals rather than comparing yourself to others. Remember, it's okay not to have everything figured out yet. Patience and self-compassion are key in navigating through these challenging times. You've got this!

patient.query:
I appreciate you saying that. It makes sense, but I still struggle to really believe it. I find it hard to celebrate my own progress because I’m always looking at what others are doing. It’s like those accomplishments just highlight how I feel stuck. 

I want to be more patient with myself, but it’s tough. I guess I just worry that if I take my time, I’ll miss out on opportunities. It feels like there’s this ticking clock in my head, and sometimes I

llm_response.response:
It's completely understandable to feel this way, as comparison can often cloud our perspective of our own progress. Remember, everyone has their own unique journey, and it's important to celebrate your own achievements, no matter how small they may seem compared to others.

Patience is key in personal growth, and while it might be challenging, taking your time to learn and grow can lead to long-lasting success rather than short-term gains that may not be sustainable. Try to reframe your mindset by focusing on progress, not perfection, and remember that opportunities will always present themselves when you're ready for them.

patient.query:
Yeah, I hear you. It just feels really hard to let go of that feeling of urgency. I guess it would be nice to focus on my own path and be okay with the pace I’m moving at. 

But sometimes, I still catch myself feeling like I’m falling behind. I wish I could just celebrate the small wins, but it’s like they don’t feel significant enough compared to what I see others doing. It’s frustrating, you know? I want to feel okay with where

llm_response.response: I completely understand how you're feeling. It's a common struggle to compare ourselves to others and feel like we're not moving fast enough. However, it's important to remember that everyone has their own unique journey and pace. Celebrating small wins is a crucial step towards self-growth and achieving larger goals. Try to focus on your progress rather than comparing yourself to others. It might take time, but with patience and persistence, you'll get there!

patient.query:
Thanks for understanding, it really does help to hear that. I know in my head that everyone has their own path, but in the moment, it’s so hard to shake off those comparisons. 

I guess I just wish it didn’t feel so heavy all the time. It would be nice to celebrate those small wins more easily, but sometimes I feel like they just slip through my fingers. Like, I’ll have a good day, but then the doubts creep back in. It’s just exhausting

llm_response.response: I completely understand where you're coming from. Comparison can often cloud our perspective and make us forget about our own progress. Remember, it's okay to take things at your own pace. Celebrating small wins is crucial for maintaining motivation and self-esteem. Try to acknowledge these victories when they happen, even if they seem insignificant in the moment. It might help to write them down or share them with someone you trust. And remember, it's normal to have doubts, but don't let them overshadow your achievements. Keep going, one step at a time. You've got this!

patient.query:
Thank you for saying that. I really do want to celebrate those small wins, but it’s like I get so caught up in what I feel I should be achieving that I forget to even recognize what I’ve done. 

Writing them down sounds interesting. I guess it could help me see things more clearly. It’s just that doubts can hit me out of nowhere, and I struggle to push them aside. I want to believe that I’m moving forward, but sometimes it feels so far away.

llm_response.response: I completely understand where you're coming from. It's easy to get lost in the pursuit of bigger goals and forget to appreciate the small victories along the way. Writing down your achievements can indeed help bring clarity and boost your morale. As for the doubts, they are a common part of the journey. Instead of trying to push them aside, you might find it helpful to acknowledge them and then focus on the progress you've made so far. Remember, every step forward, no matter how small, brings you closer to your goals. Keep going!
